original_abstract: Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education is increasingly viewed as a vehicle for global dominance and a panacea to economic downturns, environmental challenges, and food security. However, divergences in STEM education agendas at regional and national levels imply disparities in policy formulation and implementation in the Global North and Global South. This study sought to explore what informs the drivers of STEM education in the two geo-economic blocks with a view to understanding contextual factors that inform practice. A focus on STEM education in the Global North and Global South becomes necessary, given the widespread calls for collaborative work, for example, shared interests in addressing sustainable development goals, and research on the COVID-19 pandemic. A theoretical approach, based on a review of relevant literature, was adopted. Ideology critique informed the analysis and was used to make sense of the salient themes. In the Global North, STEM education is historically driven by ambitions of political dominance, the need to curb economic slumps and address critical skills shortages, and growing desire for extra-terrestrial colonization. Within this context we argue that a neoliberal agenda drives the STEM education enterprise. In the Global South, massification with equity dominates policy formulation and implementation as countries battle to redress past colonial imbalances. The Global South countries generally sign up to regional and global STEM education agendas but financial constraints compounded by an unabated brain drain result in stagnation at policy adoption at vocational level. Convenient partnerships are increasingly fashionable as countries in the Global North seek to exploit the geographical advantage of those in the Global South in order to fully utilise the extra-terrestrial space, resources for biomedical science and indigenous natural resources, among others. Collaboration endeavors between the Global North and Global South need to be mutually beneficial. The Global North needs to redistribute the aspects of power it holds in relation to STEM to move towards more equitable policies and practices across these geopolitical realms. We recommend greater vocationalisation of STEM education hinged on STEM integration with the humanities in the Global South and balanced, mutually beneficial STEM collaboration endeavors with the Global North countries.
humanized_abstract: At global level, Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) education is increasingly viewed as a means to achieve global supremacy and redress socio-economic challenges such as depression, environmental degradation and food insecurity. Nonetheless, the divergence in STEM agendas in the SADC and Africa Council for Education (ACE) countries on the continent suggests disparities in the way policies are formulated and implemented in the Global North and the Global South. It therefore becomes imperative to examine the drivers of STEM in the Global North and the Global South so as to understand the contextual issues surrounding the practice of STEM in the two geo-economic blocks. The increased interest in collaborative work such as attainment of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the study on the effects of COVID-19 makes it necessary to focus on STEM in the Global North and the Global South. This study employed a theoretical approach based on a review of literature on the drivers of STEM education. Ideology critique was employed in the analysis of the salient themes in the study. In the Global North, the drivers of STEM have been political and economic. The countries in the Global North, as explained by Duque-Garcia (2017: 3), have been driven by power, by the need to deal with economic crises and lack of skills in certain fields, and by a dream to explore outer space. Hence we argue that a neoliberal agenda is driving the STEM education enterprise in the Global North. In the Global South, the main driver of STEM education is massification with equity, which is a historical one in an attempt to address past colonial imbalances. The countries in the Global South generally adopt and also sign up to the regional and global STEM agendas but they are however unable to implement them at the vocational level due to lack of funds and the persistent brain drain. The reasons for these partnerships are diverse with countries in the Global North wanting to utilise the advantage of location in countries in the Global South such as access to outer space, biomedical science and indigenous natural resources among others. It is therefore important for these collaborative efforts between the Global North and the Global South to be mutually beneficial. It is recommended that there be more vocationalisation of STEM education in the Global South by integrating aspects of STEM with the humanities, while countries in the Global North need to redistribute power in relation to STEM so as to have more equal STEM policies and practices across the two geo-economic blocks.
